listen_1.title: EEG
listen_1.direction: Unidirectional – passive receiver capturing electric fields generated by pyramidal synaptic activity
listen_1.short_desc: EEG measures voltage fluctuations resulting from ionic current flow within neurons. Electrodes placed on the scalp capture the spatial summation of synchronous post-synaptic potentials. The signal passes through several filtering layers (cerebrospinal fluid, dura mater, bone, skin), which massively degrades spatial precision.
listen_1.mechanism: Potential difference between an active electrode and a reference electrode, amplified and filtered. Standard international 10-20 placement system (non-implanted). For intracranial EEG (iEEG/ECoG), electrodes are placed subdurally.
use_cases: Clinical: Epilepsy diagnosis, sleep monitoring, coma evaluation
BCI: Prosthetic control via sensorimotor rhythms (SMR) or evoked potentials (P300)
Research: Study of cognitive functions and functional connectivity

specs.resolution_spatial: ~1–3 cm (poor – limited by skull volume conduction)
specs.resolution_temporal: < 1 ms (excellent – captures neural dynamics in real time)
specs.bandwidth: 0.1 Hz to ~100 Hz (clinical bands: Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma)
specs.itr: 5 to 50 bits/min (very low compared to invasive methods)
specs.latency: ~5 ms to 40 ms (hardware + signal processing); biological P300 latency ~300 ms
specs.snr: Low – µV signal often buried in muscular (EMG), ocular (EOG) artifacts and line noise (50/60 Hz)
specs.cost: Consumer: $200–$1,000 (e.g., Muse, Emotiv); Research/Clinical: $10,000–$150,000 (e.g., BioSemi, Magstim)

regulatory.class: Class II (FDA/USA) or Class IIa (EU/MDR) – non-invasive diagnostic device
regulatory.phase: Phase IV (post-commercialization / standard of care since 1924)
regulatory.biocompatibility: Inert. Stable metals (Ag/AgCl, Gold) and water-based conductive gels. Risk limited to skin irritation.

listen_1.trade_off: The hidden cost of EEG is preparation time. Impedance must be kept low (< 5 kΩ), requiring gel application and scalp cleaning, making daily plug-and-play use nearly impossible without dry electrodes – which have an even lower SNR.

listen_2.title: MEG
listen_2.direction: Unidirectional – captures magnetic fields induced by intracellular currents (primarily apical dendrites)
listen_2.short_desc: MEG measures extremely weak neural magnetic fields (on the order of femtotesla, 10⁻¹⁵ T) via ultra-sensitive sensors: SQUIDs (Superconducting Quantum Interference Devices) or, more recently, OPMs (Optically Pumped Magnetometers). Unlike EEG, the magnetic signal is not distorted by the skull or scalp, offering superior spatial resolution without being invasive.
listen_2.mechanism: Detection of magnetic flux by a pickup coil coupled to a superconducting loop (SQUID), converting flux to voltage. Requires a magnetically shielded room (MSR).
use_cases: Pre-surgical localization: epileptic foci and eloquent cortex mapping before tumor resection
Cognitive neuroscience: mental chronometry (language, visual perception)
Connectivity: real-time study of neural networks

specs.channels: 150 to 306 channels (SQUID); up to 128 for OPM configurations
specs.itr: 10–50 bits/min (similar to EEG; equipment too bulky for practical daily BCI)
specs.latency: < 1 ms (acquisition); processing latency depends on inverse problem algorithm complexity
specs.snr: Extremely low at acquisition – ambient magnetic noise is 10⁹ times stronger than brain signal; SNR depends entirely on shielding quality and software filtering
specs.cost: SQUID system: $2,000,000–$4,000,000 (including shielded room and helium maintenance); OPM system: $200,000–$500,000

regulatory.phase: Phase IV – standard clinical use for epilepsy and surgery since the 1990s
regulatory.biocompatibility: Inert. Completely non-invasive, no energy emission toward the patient.

listen_2.trade_off: MEG offers the best combination of non-invasiveness and precision available. However, its operating cost is its breaking point: dependence on liquid helium (a finite and expensive resource) makes SQUID MEG economically unsustainable long-term compared to emerging OPMs, which operate at room temperature.

listen_3.title: ECoG
listen_3.direction: Primarily Listen / Potential Loop – allows direct cortical stimulation (Talk) for functional mapping or closed-loop applications
listen_3.short_desc: ECoG involves placing grids or strip electrodes directly on the cortical surface, either subdurally or epidurally. Unlike EEG, the absence of the skull's filtering effect allows capturing high-frequency signals (Gamma band, 70–150 Hz) and local field potentials (LFPs) with markedly superior spatial fidelity and SNR.
listen_3.mechanism: Electrodes capture post-synaptic potentials of local neuronal populations. Signal is routed via percutaneous cables to an external amplifier. Implanted on the cortical surface via craniotomy or burr hole.
use_cases: Epilepsy: precise localization of the epileptogenic zone before surgical resection
High-performance BCI: decoding speech and complex movements (e.g., robotic arms)
Neuroscience: study of fine cortical dynamics and cognition

specs.resolution_spatial: 1 mm to 10 mm (depends on electrode spacing)
specs.channels: 16 to 256 (clinical standard); experimental µECoG arrays exceed 1,000–5,000 channels
specs.bandwidth: 0.1 Hz to 500 Hz (clinical); up to 2 kHz (high-gamma research)
specs.itr: 20 to 100 bits/min; capable of near-natural language speed (~60–90 words/min) in recent experimental systems
specs.latency: ~10 ms to 50 ms – low electronic latency; overall depends on signal processing window (FFT, etc.)
specs.snr: High – approximately 5–10× superior to EEG due to neural proximity and absence of external muscle noise
specs.cost: Surgery + hospitalization: $50,000–$150,000; Grid material: $5,000–$20,000 per unit

regulatory.class: Class III (FDA/PMA) for chronic implants; Class II for temporary intraoperative monitoring (< 30 days)
regulatory.phase: Phase IV for clinical epilepsy; Phase I/II for permanent BCI applications (e.g., Blackrock, Synchron)
regulatory.biocompatibility: Silicone (Silastic) and Platinum/Iridium alloys. Limited glial scarring risk compared to penetrating electrodes.

listen_3.trade_off: ECoG's major compromise is its invasive nature. Despite exceptional signal quality, it requires skull opening (craniotomy), limiting its use to patients who already have a surgical indication or severe motor disabilities.

listen_4.title: fMRI
listen_4.direction: Indirect – detection of blood oxygenation variations linked to neural activity
listen_4.short_desc: fMRI relies on the BOLD effect (Blood Oxygen Level Dependent). When neurons activate, they consume oxygen, triggering a local increase in oxygenated blood flow. Oxygenated (diamagnetic) and deoxygenated (paramagnetic) hemoglobin differentially alter the local magnetic field, detected by the magnet as a T2* signal change.
listen_4.mechanism: RF excitation cycle and relaxation of hydrogen protons in a powerful static magnetic field (B₀). Detects the hemodynamic response (BOLD). Subject is positioned in a tubular scanner with a head coil surrounding the skull.
use_cases: Clinical: pre-operative functional mapping (Broca/Wernicke areas), post-stroke evaluation
Research: localization of memory, emotion and consciousness networks
Neurofeedback: real-time self-regulation of brain activity for pain or depression treatment

specs.resolution_spatial: ~1–3 mm (excellent – can visualize deep structures such as the thalamus)
specs.channels: ~50,000 to 1,000,000+ voxels (each voxel acts as an independent 3D information channel)
specs.itr: < 2 bits/min (extremely low – vascular response too slow for rapid interaction)
specs.latency: 4 to 6 seconds (intrinsic delay between neural activity and peak blood flow – hemodynamic impulse response)
specs.snr: Moderate – BOLD signal represents only 1–5% variation above background; often requires many task repetitions

regulatory.class: Class II (FDA, product code LNH) – diagnostic imaging device
regulatory.phase: Phase IV – standard reference technology in hospitals and research since the 1990s
regulatory.biocompatibility: Inert. No direct physical contact or ionizing radiation. Primary hazard: ferromagnetic projectile attraction by the magnet.

listen_4.trade_off: fMRI offers the best whole-brain overview with millimetric spatial precision without surgery. However, it is 'temporally blind': it does not see thought itself, but the metabolic waste it leaves behind with several seconds of delay.

listen_5.title: fNIRS
listen_5.direction: Indirect – measures hemoglobin concentration changes via optical properties of tissue
listen_5.short_desc: fNIRS injects photons in the near-infrared spectrum (700–900 nm) through the scalp. Biological tissues are relatively transparent in this 'optical window', but oxygenated (HbO₂) and deoxygenated (Hb) hemoglobin absorb light at specific wavelengths. By measuring reflected light (banana-shaped trajectory), local metabolic activity is inferred.
listen_5.mechanism: Photon diffusion following the modified Beer-Lambert law and detection of backscattering. Non-invasive optode cap (emitters and detectors) placed on the scalp.
use_cases: Pediatrics: study of cerebral development in infants (more movement-tolerant than MRI)
Neuromarketing / Ergonomics: measurement of mental workload in real-world conditions (walking, driving)
Rehabilitation: post-stroke recovery monitoring during physical exercise

specs.resolution_spatial: ~1–2 cm (poor – limited by photon diffusion and inter-optode distance)
specs.resolution_temporal: ~10–100 ms for acquisition; effectively limited by hemodynamic response (seconds)
specs.channels: 8 to 128+ channels (depends on number of source-detector pairs)
specs.itr: < 5 bits/min (too slow for rapid BCI commands due to vascular inertia)
specs.latency: 2 to 8 seconds (blood transit time and BOLD response)
specs.snr: Low to moderate – sensitive to hair color, skull thickness and systemic blood flow (scalp)

regulatory.phase: Phase IV – used routinely for neonatal cerebral oxygenation monitoring and in clinical research
regulatory.biocompatibility: Inert. Skin contact via plastic, silicone and optical fibers. No known risks (non-ionizing light).

listen_5.trade_off: fNIRS is often marketed as 'the poor or nomadic person's fMRI'. The hidden cost is penetration depth: light does not exceed 2–3 cm in depth, making it blind to subcortical structures (amygdala, hippocampus), limiting its use to cortical surface study.

listen_6.title: PET
listen_6.direction: Indirect / Metabolic – detects molecular and biochemical processes via radioactive tracers
listen_6.short_desc: PET relies on the injection of a radiopharmaceutical (e.g., ¹⁸F-FDG, a glucose analog). The unstable isotope emits a positron that collides with a local electron, causing annihilation. This reaction emits two 511 keV gamma photons in opposite directions (180°). A ring of detectors captures these coincidences to reconstruct a 3D map of metabolic activity or synaptic receptor density.
listen_6.mechanism: Positron-electron annihilation mechanism and temporal coincidence detection. 3D metabolic activity map reconstruction. Patient lies in a circular tunnel of scintillation detectors.
use_cases: Oncology: detection of tumors and metastases (hypermetabolism)
Neurology: differential diagnosis of dementias (Alzheimer vs. Pick's disease) and localization of interictal epileptic foci
Neuropharmacology: measurement of receptor occupancy by new drugs

specs.resolution_spatial: ~4–6 mm (medium – limited by the positron path in tissue before annihilation)
specs.resolution_temporal: Minutes (very poor – requires tracer accumulation; unsuitable for real-time thought tracking)
specs.channels: Millions of voxels (resolution depends on scintillator crystal density of the device)
specs.bandwidth: N/A – static accumulation or slow kinetics measured over several minutes
specs.itr: ~0 bits/min – unusable for motor or communication BCI control
specs.latency: 10 to 40 minutes (time for radiotracer to be captured by target tissues – incubation period)
specs.snr: Low – signal limited by acceptable radiation dose; subject to Poisson noise (random decay)
specs.cost: Equipment: $2,000,000–$5,000,000 (often combined with CT or MRI scanner); Per exam: $1,500–$4,000 (high cost due to short isotope half-life requiring a cyclotron)

regulatory.phase: Phase IV – global clinical standard for molecular imaging since the 1980s
regulatory.biocompatibility: Radiological risk. Exposure to ionizing radiation (typical dose: 5–10 mSv per exam, equivalent to several years of natural background radiation).

listen_6.trade_off: PET is the only tool capable of seeing the specific chemistry of the brain (e.g., amyloid plaques, dopamine transporters). The hidden cost is its systemic invasiveness: injecting radioactivity prevents any repeated or daily use, unlike MRI or EEG.

listen_7.title: Calcium Imaging
listen_7.direction: Indirect / Proxy – records Ca²⁺ ion fluxes as an indicator of neural activity
listen_7.short_desc: Calcium imaging uses fluorescent indicators (proteins such as GCaMP or chemical dyes) that emit light when binding to calcium. Since an action potential triggers massive Ca²⁺ entry into the neuron, the cell 'lights up' with each discharge. Two-photon or miniscope microscopes film these flashes in real time, allowing visualization of the activity of thousands of individually identified neurons.
listen_7.mechanism: Fluorescent indicator (fluorophore) + Ca²⁺ binding → conformational change → increased fluorescence excited by laser. Requires a cranial optical window (craniotomy replaced by glass) or GRIN lens inserted into the parenchyma for deep imaging.
use_cases: Fundamental research: mapping of memory and learning circuits in animal models
Drug discovery: testing the effect of neurotropic molecules on entire neural networks
Brain mapping: visualization of functional topography at the cellular scale

specs.resolution_spatial: < 1 µm (ultra-high – subcellular resolution: dendrites, synaptic spines)
specs.resolution_temporal: 50–100 ms (medium – limited by calcium binding kinetics and image capture speed)
specs.channels: 1,000 to 10,000+ neurons simultaneously in a single field of view
specs.bandwidth: Extremely high (Gbps) – high-resolution video stream at 30–100 fps generates terabytes of raw data per hour
specs.itr: Potentially very high (> 500 bits/min theoretically) as precise identity of each network neuron is accessible
specs.latency: ~50 to 150 ms – delay due to slow calcium signal rise after electrical spike and camera integration time
specs.snr: High in superficial layers – very contrasted signal vs. dark background; attenuates with depth due to photon scattering
specs.cost: Two-photon microscope: $250,000–$600,000; Miniscope (portable): $5,000–$20,000; Indicators (viral vectors): $500–$2,000 per injection

regulatory.class: N/A – research use only; not approved for humans as it requires genetic modification (viral vector) or chemical injection
regulatory.phase: Pre-clinical – essentially limited to animal models (mice, non-human primates)
regulatory.biocompatibility: Problematic. Risk of phototoxicity (laser tissue burning), calcium saturation (disruption of cellular homeostasis) and immune reaction to the viral vector.

listen_7.trade_off: Calcium imaging offers ground truth on cell identity (knowing exactly which type of neuron activates). However, its hidden cost is its biological asymmetry: a slow and persistent chemical response is observed, preventing capture of the precise millisecond timing of electrical discharges that only electrophysiology can capture.

talk_1.title: TMS
talk_1.direction: Unidirectional – injects electromagnetic energy to modulate neural activity
talk_1.short_desc: TMS is based on Faraday's law of induction: a coil placed on the scalp generates a brief, powerful pulsed magnetic field (~1.5 to 3 Tesla). This field passes through the skull painlessly and induces an electric field in the underlying cortical tissue, causing neuron depolarization and action potential firing.
talk_1.mechanism: High-voltage capacitor discharge into a coil → rapidly varying magnetic field → induced eddy current in the brain cortex. Figure-of-eight coil typically held against the scalp, targeting the motor cortex or dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC).
use_cases: Psychiatry: treatment of treatment-resistant major depression (rTMS), OCD and smoking cessation
Neurology: post-stroke rehabilitation, treatment of migraines and chronic pain
Research: mapping of cortical excitability and probing brain-behavior causal relationships

specs.resolution_spatial: ~1–2 cm (medium – limited by coil geometry and current diffusion)
specs.resolution_temporal: < 1 ms (excellent for pulse); biological effects can last minutes to months (plasticity)
specs.channels: Generally 1 channel (one target at a time); experimental systems use 4–16 coil arrays
specs.bandwidth: N/A – stimulation frequency: 1 Hz for inhibition (inhibitory rTMS), 10–50 Hz for excitation
specs.itr: N/A – not used for binary data transfer but for modulating brain states
specs.latency: Instantaneous (physical); induced motor response (MEP) latency ~20–25 ms for hand
specs.snr: N/A (Talk) – 'targeting reliability' measured, often assisted by MRI neuronavigation to reduce placement error

regulatory.class: Class II (FDA, product code OBW) – neurological stimulation device
regulatory.phase: Phase IV – FDA-approved standard treatment for depression since 2008
regulatory.biocompatibility: Excellent (non-invasive). Risks: mild headaches, skin discomfort, and rare risk of epileptic seizure (< 0.1%).

talk_1.trade_off: TMS allows testing causality (virtually 'switching off' an area) non-invasively. The hidden cost is cellular non-specificity: the magnetic field stimulates everything under the coil (excitatory neurons, inhibitory neurons, and passing fibers), making the net result sometimes unpredictable without simultaneous EEG monitoring.

talk_2.title: tDCS / tACS
talk_2.direction: Unidirectional – sub-threshold neuromodulation: does not trigger action potentials but modifies discharge probability
talk_2.short_desc: tDCS delivers a weak direct current (1–2 mA) via electrodes on the scalp. The anode depolarizes the membrane (increases excitability), while the cathode hyperpolarizes it (reduces it). tACS uses alternating current to synchronize or 'entrain' endogenous neural oscillations at a specific frequency (e.g., Alpha, Gamma).
talk_2.mechanism: Ionic current flow through the skull (volume conduction) modifying the resting membrane potential. Non-invasive sponge electrodes (soaked in saline) placed on the scalp.
use_cases: Psychiatry: major depression (alternative or adjunct to TMS)
Rehabilitation: improvement of post-stroke plasticity (motor and language)
Research / Performance: modulation of working memory and attention

specs.resolution_spatial: > 2–3 cm (very poor – current diffuses widely in the scalp before penetrating the skull)
specs.resolution_temporal: High for application; effects are neuromodulatory and slow (synaptic plasticity)
specs.channels: Generally 2 (Anode/Cathode); HD-tDCS uses 5–8 small electrodes to improve focality
specs.bandwidth: tDCS: 0 Hz (DC); tACS: 0.1 Hz to ~200 Hz
specs.itr: N/A – not used for binary data transfer but for changing network state
specs.latency: Instantaneous (physical membrane effect); behavioral effects appear after 10–20 minutes of stimulation
specs.snr: N/A (Talk) – 'focality' of induced electric field (V/m) evaluated relative to non-targeted areas
specs.cost: Consumer/DIY: $150–$500 (e.g., Flow Neuroscience); Research/Clinical: $5,000–$15,000 (e.g., Neuroelectrics, Soterix)

regulatory.class: Class II (FDA) for certain indications (pain/depression); Class IIa (EU/MDR)
regulatory.phase: Phase IV for pain and depression; Phase II/III for cognitive disorders and Alzheimer's disease in 2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Excellent. Minor risks: tingling, skin redness (erythema) under electrode, sensation of light flashes (phosphenes) with tACS.

talk_2.trade_off: The major advantage is negligible cost and portability (enabling home treatment). The hidden cost is dosing uncertainty: skull thickness and gyri morphology vary so much between individuals that current actually reaching the brain can vary by a factor of 3 for the same device settings.

talk_3.title: ICMS
talk_3.direction: Unidirectional / Direct – injection of micro-currents directly into brain parenchyma to trigger action potentials
talk_3.short_desc: ICMS uses micro-electrode arrays (often Utah Arrays) penetrating the cortex to a depth of 1.5 mm. By applying biphasic low-intensity electrical pulses (µA), it directly excites cell bodies or axons near the electrode tip. This allows 'short-circuiting' natural sensory inputs to inject artificial information directly into cortical processing.
talk_3.mechanism: Electrode-electrolyte interface model and capacitive/Faradaic charge injection. Electrodes implanted in the granular/infragranular layers of the cortex (e.g., S1 for touch, V1 for vision).
use_cases: Sensory restoration: creation of touch sensations (proprioception and pressure) for robotic prosthesis users
Visual prosthetics: stimulation of primary visual cortex (V1) to generate phosphenes (light points) for blind individuals
Pain treatment: direct modulation of somatosensory circuits

specs.resolution_temporal: < 1 ms (ultra-high – can mimic precise timing of natural spikes)
specs.channels: 96 to 128 channels per array (Utah Array type); multi-array systems up to 512+ channels
specs.bandwidth: Typical stimulation frequencies of 10 Hz to 300 Hz
specs.itr: 10 to 40 bits/sec (in terms of injected sensory discrimination capacity)
specs.latency: < 5 ms (electronic); human perception is near-instantaneous upon current injection
specs.snr: N/A (Talk) – 'detection threshold' (current required to elicit sensation) is measured
specs.cost: Material (Utah Array): ~$15,000–$25,000 per unit; Surgery and follow-up: > $100,000

regulatory.phase: Phase I/II (early clinical trials under IDE exemption) – primarily used in research programs such as 'BrainGate'
regulatory.biocompatibility: Critical. Fibrous encapsulation (gliosis) after 6–12 months increases impedance and may require current increase (potential neurotoxicity).

talk_3.trade_off: ICMS offers ultimate granularity for speaking to the brain. However, its hidden cost is microscopic tissue destruction: physical insertion of silicon tips creates permanent scarring. Long-term, the interface may lose efficacy if target neurons migrate away from the electrode due to gliosis.

talk_4.title: VNS
talk_4.direction: Unidirectional (Talk, classic) / Loop (modern 'Sensing' systems detect tachycardia to trigger reactive stimulation)
talk_4.short_desc: VNS stimulates the vagus nerve (typically left cervical) to modulate brain networks at a distance. The vagus nerve is a major afferent 'highway' to the brainstem (nucleus tractus solitarius), which then projects to the thalamus and cortex. Stimulation modifies neurotransmitter release (noradrenaline, serotonin) and desynchronizes cortical activity to stop seizures or stabilize mood.
talk_4.mechanism: Stimulator → vagal afferents → brainstem → ascending activation system → diffuse cortical modulation. Impulse generator (IPG) placed under the left clavicle; helical electrode wraps around the left vagus nerve in the neck.
use_cases: Epilepsy: reduction of seizure frequency in pharmaco-resistant patients
Depression: treatment of chronic or recurrent treatment-resistant depression (TRD)
Obesity: blocking of hunger signals (VBLOC)
Rehabilitation: improvement of post-stroke plasticity (stimulation/rehabilitation coupling)

specs.resolution_spatial: Very poor (systemic) – no precise brain area targeted; induces a global brain state change
specs.resolution_temporal: High (ms for pulse); therapeutic response (mood) can take weeks
specs.channels: 1 channel (single bipolar or tripolar electrode around the nerve)
specs.bandwidth: Stimulation frequencies: 20–30 Hz (standard); pulse width: 250–500 µs
specs.itr: N/A – no binary data transfer to/from brain
specs.latency: ~ms (physical) to brainstem; clinical effect on seizure can take 30–60 seconds
specs.snr: N/A (Talk); in 'Sensing' models, ECG detection SNR is high thanks to integrated filtering
specs.cost: Total (surgery + device): $30,000–$50,000; periodic adjustment fees by neurologist

regulatory.phase: Phase IV – clinical use since 1997 for epilepsy and 2005 for depression
regulatory.biocompatibility: Titanium for IPG, silicone/platinum for electrodes. Risks: hoarseness, cervical pain, and bradycardia (rare).

talk_4.trade_off: VNS is the ideal 'backdoor': it allows modulating the brain without ever opening the skull. However, its hidden cost is functional non-specificity. By stimulating the vagus nerve, it does not only stimulate the brain: it also influences the heart, lungs and digestive system, explaining the frequent voice side effects (hoarseness) during stimulation phases.

talk_5.title: tFUS
talk_5.direction: Unidirectional – non-invasive neuromodulation using acoustic mechanical energy
talk_5.short_desc: tFUS uses low-intensity ultrasound waves focused through the skull toward millimetric brain targets. Unlike TMS, it can reach deep structures (thalamus, hippocampus) without surgery. The mechanism relies on mechanotransduction: acoustic pressure modifies neural membrane capacitance and activates mechanosensitive ion channels, modulating excitability.
talk_5.mechanism: Focusing of ultrasound waves creating a high acoustic pressure zone (focus) at the beam convergence point. Transducers placed on the scalp, often integrated in a water-cooled helmet for wave coupling and skin protection.
use_cases: Thermal ablation (high intensity): treatment of essential tremors and Parkinson's disease
Neuromodulation (low intensity): chronic pain, epilepsy, OCD
Blood-brain barrier (BBB) opening: targeted drug / chemotherapy delivery

specs.resolution_spatial: ~2–5 mm (excellent – best non-invasive resolution for deep targets)
specs.resolution_temporal: High (ms) – pulses can be synchronized with brain rhythms
specs.channels: 1 (fixed transducer) to 1024 elements (multi-element arrays with electronic focus correction through skull)
specs.bandwidth: Carrier frequencies: 200 kHz to 1 MHz; pulse repetition frequency (PRF): up to 1 kHz
specs.itr: N/A – used for modulation of pathological or functional states
specs.latency: ~ms (physical wave propagation delay) followed by near-instantaneous neural response
specs.snr: N/A (Talk) – pressure ratio between focus and lateral lobes (undesired heating zones) is measured

regulatory.class: Class III (FDA/PMA) for thermal ablation; Class II for research neuromodulation
regulatory.phase: Phase IV for essential tremor; Phase II/III for BBB opening and depression in 2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Excellent (non-invasive). Risks: skull bone heating, intracranial cavitation (gas bubbles) and micro-hemorrhages if intensity is too high.

talk_5.trade_off: tFUS is the only technology capable of stimulating deep brain structures (e.g., amygdala) with electrode-like precision without drilling the skull. The hidden cost is physical complexity: skull geometry and thickness vary so much between individuals that they deflect the beam (aberration), requiring ultra-complex and costly phase correction algorithms to avoid burning healthy areas.

talk_6.title: Magnetothermal Stimulation
talk_6.direction: Unidirectional – remote control of neural activity via magneto-caloric energy conversion
talk_6.short_desc: This technique uses magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) injected into brain tissue and targeted to specific neurons (often via genetic expression of thermosensitive ion channels such as TRPV1). When an external alternating magnetic field (AMF) is applied, nanoparticles heat up via hysteresis losses. This local temperature rise (~2–5°C) opens TRPV1 channels, causing calcium entry and action potential triggering.
talk_6.mechanism: Alternating magnetic field → agitation of MNP magnetic moments → heat → TRPV1 channel activation → ionic influx. Nanoparticles injected in situ into brain parenchyma; AMF coils surround the head or animal cage.
use_cases: Wireless DBS: stimulation of deep structures (e.g., striatum) without permanent electrodes or cables
Behavioral study: modulation of specific circuits in freely moving animals within a magnetic field
Targeted therapy: potential treatment of motor or psychiatric diseases with reduced long-term invasiveness

specs.resolution_spatial: Excellent (cellular) – limited only by genetic expression of the channel and nanoparticle diffusion
specs.resolution_temporal: Low (seconds) – limited by thermal inertia (heating and heat dissipation time)
specs.channels: Potential multiplexing: using nanoparticles with different resonance frequencies could theoretically stimulate several neuronal populations independently
specs.bandwidth: AMF frequency: 100–500 kHz; biological response limited by thermal cycle
specs.itr: N/A – used to modulate reward or motor circuits, not for binary data transfer
specs.latency: 2 to 10 seconds (time for MNPs to reach TRPV1 channel thermal activation threshold)
specs.snr: N/A (Talk) – activation efficiency (percentage of neurons responding to field) is measured
specs.cost: Nanoparticles: $500–$2,000 per research batch; AMF generator: $20,000–$50,000

regulatory.class: N/A (Research) – currently unclassified for humans; would likely be Class III (injectable implant + genetic modification)
regulatory.phase: Pre-clinical (rodent models) – no human trials as of 2026 due to requirement for gene therapy to express TRPV1 channels
regulatory.biocompatibility: Moderate to risky. Iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe₃O₄) are biodegradable, but chronic accumulation and repeated heating can induce neuroinflammation or cell death by hyperthermia.

talk_6.trade_off: Magnetothermal stimulation solves the brain 'wiring' problem: once injected, the subject is free of all physical connections. However, the hidden cost is thermal kinetics: unlike optogenetics or electricity, stimulation cannot be stopped instantaneously. Heat takes time to dissipate, potentially causing prolonged unwanted activations or tissue damage if the duty cycle is poorly managed.

talk_7.title: Optogenetics
talk_7.direction: Unidirectional / Direct / Cellular – injects signals into specific neuronal populations with binary On/Off precision
talk_7.short_desc: Optogenetics combines genetic engineering and optics to control cellular activity. A viral vector (generally AAV) inserts genes coding for light-sensitive proteins (opsins, such as Channelrhodopsin) into targeted neurons. Once expressed, these proteins act as ion channels that open or close in response to light pulses (lasers or LEDs), enabling instantaneous activation or inhibition of neurons.
talk_7.mechanism: Photon → Opsin → ionic flux (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) → action potential. Implanted either in the retina (intravitreal injection) or in the cortex/deep structures (optical fibers).
use_cases: Vision restoration: treatment of retinitis pigmentosa and Stargardt disease (converting ganglion cells into photosensitive cells)
Hearing: optical cochlear implants for better frequency resolution than electrical implants
Neurology / Psychiatry: research on control of epileptic seizures, chronic pain and Parkinson's disease

specs.resolution_spatial: < 1 µm (ultra-high, cellular) – can target a single neuron via two-photon holography
specs.resolution_temporal: < 1 ms (excellent) – can mimic natural neural codes at high frequency
specs.channels: 1 (simple optical fiber) to > 10,000 neurons controlled independently via µLED arrays or spatial holography
specs.bandwidth: Stimulation frequencies up to 300 Hz (with fast variants like Chronos or f-ChR2 TC)
specs.itr: Potentially very high (> 1,000 bits/sec) – limited only by complexity of light projection system (DMD/Holography)
specs.snr: Very high – activation is binary and only affects cells expressing the opsin, eliminating interference with non-targeted neighboring neurons
specs.cost: Research: laser systems $80,000–$150,000; AAV vectors ~$1.2 M for initial clinical preparation; Therapy (estimate): $300,000–$500,000 per eye (similar to gene therapies such as Luxturna)

regulatory.class: Class III (FDA/MDR) – combination of gene therapy (biological) and light stimulation device (hardware)
regulatory.phase: Phase I/II (human trials ongoing for vision: GenSight, Bionic Sight, Nanoscope Therapeutics). MCO-010 reported pivot Phase 2 results in 2024–2025
regulatory.biocompatibility: Risky. Requires permanent genetic modification; risks of immune inflammation against viral proteins and phototoxicity (tissue heating by light).

talk_7.trade_off: Optogenetics is the most precise communication tool ever created, capable of 'playing piano' with neurons. However, its hidden cost is genetic invasiveness: to use it, the patient must be partially transformed into a GMO, raising massive ethical and regulatory barriers compared to purely physical methods like TMS or ECoG.

talk_8.title: DREADDs
talk_8.title_in: Chemogenetics – Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs
talk_8.direction: Unidirectional / Indirect / Modulation – modifies neural excitability over long periods via specific chemical activation
talk_8.short_desc: Chemogenetics uses modified receptors (DREADDs) that no longer respond to natural neurotransmitters. They are activated only by a normally inert 'key' molecule, such as Clozapine N-oxide (CNO). Once the gene is inserted by a viral vector, injection of the molecule specifically activates or inhibits targeted neurons.
talk_8.mechanism: Viral vector → DREADD receptor expression → Ligand (CNO) administration → G-protein signaling cascade → membrane modulation. 'Implantation' via viral vector (AAV) injection into brain parenchyma; no permanent hardware interface required.
use_cases: Research: reversible circuit deactivation (e.g., amygdala in fear) without cables or optical fibers
Epilepsy: targeted inhibition of epileptic foci triggered only upon drug intake
Metabolic disorders: control of hunger and obesity circuits

specs.resolution_spatial: Very high (cellular) – depends on specificity of the genetic promoter used
specs.resolution_temporal: Very low (minutes to hours) – depends on pharmacokinetics of the activating molecule
specs.channels: Global / thousands of cells – all cells expressing the receptor are modulated simultaneously by the molecule
specs.bandwidth: N/A – tonic modulation (state maintenance) rather than rapid signal transfer
specs.latency: 15 to 60 minutes (time for molecule to cross the blood-brain barrier and reach receptors)
specs.snr: High – activating molecule designed to have no effect on other receptors in the body (biological inertness)
specs.cost: Viral vectors: $500–$2,500 per research injection; Ligand (CNO): ~$50/gram (very low)

regulatory.class: N/A (Biological Product / Gene Therapy) – falls under drug/gene therapy regulations (FDA/EMA), not standard medical device classification
regulatory.phase: Pre-clinical – widely used in rodents and non-human primates; Phase I discussions for focal epilepsy ongoing in 2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Moderate. Risks from viral vector use (immune response) and potential metabolic conversion of CNO to clozapine (an active molecule).

talk_8.trade_off: Chemogenetics enables 'DBS without wires or batteries' with cellular precision. However, its hidden cost is total lack of fine temporal control: once the drug is administered, the brain remains modulated for hours. Unlike optogenetics, the effect cannot be switched off instantaneously, limiting its use to chronic state changes.

loop_1.title: Micro-electrodes
loop_1.direction: Bidirectional / Direct – capable of recording individual action potentials (spikes) and injecting micro-currents for intracortical stimulation (ICMS)
loop_1.short_desc: The Utah Array is a rigid silicon micro-electrode array, often compared to a 'bed of nails'. It typically comprises 96–128 needles of 1–1.5 mm length. Unlike ECoG which stays on the surface, the Utah Array penetrates the parenchyma to be in close proximity to cell bodies. It is the gold standard for the highest possible signal fidelity (Single Unit Activity), enabling decoding of complex motor intentions or phonemes with near-natural precision.
loop_1.mechanism: Isolated needles (except at the tip) inserted into the cortex (motor, somatosensory or visual) via a pneumatic inserter after craniotomy → capture of the transmembrane potential difference of adjacent neurons.
use_cases: Motor control: driving robotic arms with multiple degrees of freedom and fine finger control
Speech restoration: decoding pre-motor cortex activity to synthesize speech in real time
Artificial sensation: somatosensory cortex stimulation to 'feel' the pressure of a prosthetic hand

specs.resolution_spatial: 30–50 µm (absolute – can distinguish two neighboring neurons)
specs.channels: 96 to 128 per chip; multi-chip configurations reach 1024 channels in 2026
specs.bandwidth: ~10–50 Mbps (for 100 channels at 30 kHz / 16-bit) – requires heavy compute infrastructure
specs.itr: 150 to 300 bits/min (in mental typing or speech decoding tasks)
specs.latency: < 10 ms (near-zero hardware latency; system latency depends on AI decoder used)
specs.snr: Exceptional (SNR > 10) – best available ratio for extracellular signals
specs.cost: Material (implant only): ~$15,000–$25,000; Complete system (amplifiers, software, surgery): > $150,000

regulatory.phase: Phase II (pivot trials ongoing) – used for 20 years under IDE exemption; Blackrock's MoveAgain system received 'Breakthrough Device' designation and is in final commercial validation in 2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Limited. Rigid needle friction against soft tissue creates chronic inflammation (gliosis) degrading signal after 2–5 years.

loop_1.trade_off: Utah Array is the 'Formula 1' of BCIs: unmatched performance for control and decoding. However, its hidden cost is microscopic necrosis: insertion is a trauma, and silicon rigidity eventually 'kills' the neurons it is meant to listen to, often limiting implant useful life to a few years.

loop_2.title: Neural Dust
loop_2.direction: Bidirectional – designed for listening (Listen); recent versions like StimDust enable stimulation (Talk), creating closed loops for 'electroceuticals'
loop_2.short_desc: Neural Dust consists of thousands of millimetric or microscopic wireless, battery-free sensors (motes). Each mote contains a piezoelectric crystal that converts ultrasound emitted by an external interrogator into electricity. Neural activity modifies mote electrode impedance, which alters ultrasound reflection (backscatter). The interrogator decodes this signal to reconstruct electrical activity.
loop_2.mechanism: Piezoelectric conversion of ultrasound into energy and communication via backscattering. Motes placed directly on peripheral nerves or scattered in the cerebral cortex, beneath the dura mater.
use_cases: Organ monitoring: real-time monitoring of cardiac, renal or digestive function
Peripheral nerve interface: bladder control, appetite suppression or sleep apnea treatment
Chronic BCI: long-duration cerebral recording without infectious complications of percutaneous wires

specs.channels: Theoretically unlimited (> 10,000) due to distributed wireless nature of motes
specs.bandwidth: ~10–100 kbps per mote; ASIC receiving systems can aggregate up to 1.3 Gb/s for thousands of channels
specs.itr: 30 to 150 bits/min (estimated in BCI network configuration)
specs.latency: < 10 ms (dominated by ultrasound travel time and backscatter signal processing)
specs.snr: High (~5–10, similar to high-quality wired electrodes due to immediate neural proximity)
specs.cost: Individual (estimate): $10–$150 per mote in mass production; Complete system: > $100,000 (including ASIC interrogator and implantation)

regulatory.phase: Pre-clinical / Phase I (Early Feasibility) – in vivo tests validated in rodents and primates; first human trials targeting peripheral nerves in 2025–2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Surgical-grade epoxy coatings or thin polymer films. Reduces gliosis as motes are 'floating' and do not pull on tissue.

loop_2.trade_off: Neural Dust promises an 'invisible' and eternal brain interface. However, its hidden cost is the acoustic barrier: ultrasound does not pass through bone. For the brain, this imposes either a sub-skull implanted interrogator or a permanent acoustic window, which limits the promise of total non-invasiveness.

loop_3.title: DBS
loop_3.direction: Unidirectional (Talk, classic) / Loop (recent 'Sensing' models adjust stimulation based on neural biomarkers in closed loop)
loop_3.short_desc: DBS consists of surgically implanting electrodes in deep brain structures (often the basal ganglia such as the subthalamic nucleus). These electrodes are connected to an impulse generator (IPG) placed under the clavicle. High-frequency current (~130 Hz) 'blocks' or modulates the abnormal signals responsible for motor disorders.
loop_3.mechanism: IPG circuit delivering chronic electrical pulses to target neurons. Electrodes housed in deep structures (STN, GPi or VIM) with subcutaneous extension to the chest.
use_cases: Motor: Parkinson's disease, essential tremor, dystonia
Psychiatric: OCD, Tourette's syndrome, treatment-resistant depression (experimental)
Epilepsy: stimulation of the anterior nucleus of the thalamus

specs.resolution_spatial: ~2–5 mm (medium – activated tissue volume depends on current intensity)
specs.resolution_temporal: Excellent (ms) – continuous or pulsed high-frequency stimulation
specs.channels: 4 to 8 contacts per electrode (1 or 2 electrodes per patient); directional models allow field segmentation
specs.bandwidth: Stimulation frequency: 2 Hz to 250 Hz; pulse width: 60–450 µs
specs.itr: N/A – used for functional modulation, not binary data transfer
specs.latency: Instantaneous (electrical effect); motor symptom improvement often visible within seconds of activation
specs.snr: High for 'Sensing' models (e.g., Medtronic Percept) – capable of filtering stimulation artifact to read local field potentials (LFP)

regulatory.class: Class III (FDA/PMA) – life-sustaining or high-risk implantable device
regulatory.phase: Phase IV for motor disorders and OCD; Phase II/III for Alzheimer's disease and depression in 2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Platinum/Iridium electrodes, Titanium implant body. Risk of infection (3–5%) and electrode displacement.

loop_3.trade_off: DBS is the 'brain pacemaker', offering unmatched efficacy for severe motor disorders. The hidden cost is its surgical rigidity: once implanted, repositioning is impossible without new surgery. Additionally, chronic stimulation can induce cognitive or mood side effects (e.g., impulsivity) from current diffusion into adjacent limbic structures.

loop_4.title: Stentrodes
loop_4.direction: Bidirectional – designed initially for recording (Listen); capable of electrical stimulation (Talk) for sensory feedback or activity modulation
loop_4.short_desc: The Stentrode is an endovascular brain-machine interface: a self-expanding nitinol stent with fixed micro-electrodes. Unlike conventional interfaces, it requires no craniotomy – it is inserted via the jugular vein and routed through blood vessels to the superior sagittal sinus, adjacent to the motor cortex. Once deployed, it integrates into the vessel wall (endothelialization), recording neural signals through the vascular wall without direct parenchyma contact.
loop_4.mechanism: Endovascular ECoG signal capture → transmission via intravascular cable to implanted telemetry unit (ITU) in the chest → wireless transmission to an external receiver.
use_cases: Severe paralysis (ALS, stroke, spinal cord injuries): cursor control, mental typing (email, SMS) and internet navigation
Neuroprosthetics: exoskeleton or robotic prosthetic control
Future potential: epilepsy treatment (seizure detection and suppression) and Parkinson's disease

specs.resolution_spatial: ~1–2 mm (moderate – less precise than intracortical electrodes as signal is filtered through vessel wall, but better than EEG)
specs.resolution_temporal: < 1 ms (excellent – captures Gamma band frequencies > 70 Hz)
specs.bandwidth: ~100–500 kbps (sufficient for cursor control and digital clicks)
specs.itr: 10 to 20 bits/min (~14–20 characters per minute in trained patients)
specs.latency: < 50 ms (signal acquisition); user-perceived latency depends on decoder software processing
specs.snr: High (SNR ~4–8) – protected from external interference by skull, though slightly attenuated by electrode-neuron distance
specs.cost: > $150,000 (estimate for complete system including minimally invasive surgery and telemetry unit)

regulatory.class: Class III (FDA/PMA pending) – currently under Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)
regulatory.phase: Phase I/II (Early Feasibility Study) – COMMAND (USA) and SWITCH (Australia) trials ongoing; estimated end of main feasibility study: September 2026
regulatory.biocompatibility: Excellent. Nitinol and Platinum-Iridium. Endothelialization (vessel cell coverage) reduces thrombosis risk and mechanically stabilizes the implant.

loop_4.trade_off: Stentrode is the ultimate compromise between invasiveness and performance. It avoids open-brain surgery (craniotomy), drastically reducing infection and rejection risks. However, its hidden cost is spatial limitation: confined to blood vessels, it cannot be placed anywhere in the brain, limiting use to areas 'accessible' by the vascular network (primarily motor cortex).
